target.title: Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save
target.body:
# OpenClaw session-store path fix, 2026-04-12

## Status
Applied live to installed runtime. Also ported carefully into a fresh source clone of the matching `v2026.4.11` upstream tag, but not yet merged upstream or installed from that source tree.

## Source clone
- Repo: `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/external/openclaw-upstream`
- Branch: `fix/session-store-transcript-path-canonicalization`

## Protected file
`/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/store-Cw5RrVCw.js`

## Backup
`/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/backups/runtime-patches/store-Cw5RrVCw.js.pre-20260412-1233`

## What changed
Added a save-time canonicalization step so persisted `sessionFile` and `transcriptPath` values are rewritten into the real sessions directory before session-store writes hit disk.

## Why
Without this, restored/recovered session entries could keep workspace-style transcript paths, then later be treated as missing/orphaned and get swept or misread.

## Verification performed
- syntax check passed
- synthetic save test passed
- gateway restarted cleanly
- post-restart audit:
  - `entries 913`
  - `legacy_non_agent_keys 0`
  - `non_cron_duplicate_sessionids 0`
  - `missing_jsonl_paths 0`

## Release blocker rule
Treat this as a release blocker until the fix is confirmed upstream and in the installed version. Before any OpenClaw update or manual overwrite touching runtime/config/session storage, stop and verify this protection first.

## Source patch details
Changed files in the source clone:
- `src/config/sessions/store.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
- `src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`

Validation in source clone:
- `node --no-maglev node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/config/sessions/store.pruning.integration.test.ts`
- Result: `2 passed`, `29 passed`

## Patch export
- Mailbox-style patch export written for handoff/cherry-pick use:
  - `/Users/rocklobster/.openclaw/workspace/patches/openclaw-session-store-path-fix-2026-04-12.patch`

## Current git state
- Branch is clean and still anchored on tag base `v2026.4.11`
- Head commit:
  - `e1c0d1a` `Fix session transcript path canonicalization on save`

## Next upstream action
Open or stage an upstream PR from the source clone using commit `e1c0d1a` or the exported patch above. Keep the live runtime bandage in place until an official release is installed and re-verified on this machine.

target.title: feat(discord): add canvas-first Discord Activities support
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

- Problem: Discord Activity launch/setup in OpenClaw required brittle manual component payloads, had weak agent guidance, and failed command deploys when Discord required retaining the Entry Point command.
- Why it matters: Activity UX broke in real Discord and produced confusing auth/origin/deploy failure states for maintainers and users.
- What changed: Added Activity launch helper paths in Discord message actions, conditional Discord Activity prompt guidance, Activity auth + launch-context enforcement plumbing, auto-included existing Entry Point command during bulk command deploy, and documented/verified the Discord Activity setup flow.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new standalone backend OAuth exchange endpoint was added; OAuth exchange remains app/backend-owned.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Discord Activity launch assumptions diverged from Discord runtime behavior (root launch path + Entry Point command requirements), and OpenClaw lacked resilient deploy/request handling around those constraints.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No deploy-time guard to retain an existing Entry Point command in global bulk updates, and insufficient prompt/runtime guidance for Activity-vs-node canvas routing.
- Contributing context (if known): Discord iframe/origin constraints and launch metadata requirements were not first-class in the earlier flow.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server.canvas-auth.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/system-prompt-params.test.ts`
  - `src/canvas-host/server.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Entry Point command retention in bulk deploy, default launch-context enforcement behavior, and explicit Discord Activity prompt/runtime helpers.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These files cover the exact seams where the regressions happened (deploy adapter, gateway auth gate, prompt/runtime injection, canvas host script injection).
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Expanded/updated tests above include new and adapted assertions.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Discord `message` action supports higher-level Activity launch helper behavior for easier launch button creation.
- Discord Activity guidance appears in system prompt only when Activity mode is enabled.
- `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext` is now treated as default-on (`true` unless explicitly `false`).
- Canvas-hosted pages expose `window.OpenClaw.discord` / `window.openclawDiscord` helper APIs (lazy SDK load).
- Discord Activities docs now include URL mapping/auth/troubleshooting updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens /] -> [gateway root/control-ui or auth mismatch] -> [failure]

After:
[launch-activity button] -> [Discord opens mapped /__openclaw__/canvas] -> [activity auth + launch-context checks] -> [canvas loads]
                         \-> [bulk command deploy] -> [auto-include existing Entry Point command] -> [deploy succeeds]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Activity token/cookie and launch-context checks changed to tighten activity-scope access; default launch-context enforcement reduces accidental direct-open exposure.
  - Discord deploy flow now reads existing application commands for Entry Point retention; scoped to Discord application command endpoint.
  - Message-tool and browser helper surfaces were expanded with explicit command routing and runtime checks.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw runtime (Node 22+)
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): `canvasHost.activity.enabled=true`, `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=true`, Discord account configured

### Steps

1. Configure Discord Activity URL mapping and OpenClaw activity settings.
2. Launch via Discord `launch-activity` button and observe canvas/auth behavior.
3. Start Discord provider and verify native command deploy behavior when Entry Point command exists.

### Expected

- Activity launches to canvas route and passes intended auth gates.
- Native command deploy retains Entry Point command and does not fail with 50240.

### Actual

- Verified in updated tests and runtime flow for this branch.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran targeted tests covering deploy flow, gateway auth flow, prompt/runtime guidance, and canvas helper injection.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Default-on launch-context enforcement and explicit opt-out behavior.
  - Entry Point command retention path and 50240 handling path.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full live Discord OAuth backend token-exchange endpoint flow (out of scope for this PR).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Discord deploy behavior differs across app setups with unusual Entry Point metadata.
  - Mitigation: Entry Point payload is sourced from Discord's existing command record and included only when missing from outgoing bulk payload.
- Risk: Launch-context default-on may block intentionally direct/manual canvas opens for activity mode.
  - Mitigation: Explicit opt-out via `canvasHost.activity.requireLaunchContext=false`.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.
- Ran `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: Copied local command output from the after-patch run:

```text
format:check src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts: passed
pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:
  src/agents/failover-error.test.ts
  src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
  src/agents/model-fallback.probe.test.ts
  src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts
result: 314 assertions passed
```

Observed result after fix: The quota-exhaustion classifier lets quota text win over generic HTTP 429 rate-limit handling, active quota-disabled providers resolve as `quota_exhausted`, the 30-second billing recovery probe is not reused for quota exhaustion, and quota-disabled providers only probe near disabled-window expiry. The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.

target.title: feat: Rust/GTK4 Linux companion app (#75, alternative track)
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a native Linux desktop companion under `apps/linux-gtk/` written in **Rust + GTK4 + libadwaita**
- Alternative-track contribution for [#75](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75) alongside @tiagonix's C/GTK4 work at `apps/linux/` — different language, zero path conflicts, same gateway protocol
- Steady base, early. Needs security review, packaging, and UI polish

## Why another one

I started on a prototype using web-tech (Electron) before reading through #75, then saw [@steipete's comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75#issuecomment-3747113141):

> My vision for this is a cute gtk-native Linux app. Esp for the statusbar features.

Switched tracks to native GTK. @tiagonix's C implementation was the reference, but I wanted to see what the Rust ecosystem gives us here (borrow-checked gateway client, fewer NULL pointer surprises, tokio for async RPC, `cargo test` for unit coverage).

Happy to fold it in, leave it as a community showcase, or archive it — whichever serves the project.

## What's included

**Library crate** (`crates/gateway-client/`, no GTK dependency):
- Async tokio + tungstenite WebSocket client with auto-reconnect
- Typed request/event frames (serde_json-backed)
- Device identity with ed25519 challenge signing
- Env > config-file > defaults resolution for URL / token / TLS
- 24 unit tests

**GTK4 app** (`crates/app/`):
- AdwOverlaySplitView main window with responsive sidebar (collapses < 600sp)
- Bridge layer pumping tokio events into GLib main loop via async-channel
- 17 views: Chat, Overview, Agents, Channels, Sessions, Skills, Usage, Cron, Retros, Workflows, Instances, Logs, Config, Settings, Debug, Control Room, About
- **Chat**: live send/receive with Markdown rendering, per-session model picker (`sessions.patch`), typing indicator, thinking/tool-call toggles, history via `chat.history`
- **Agents**: master/detail with editable settings (`agents.update`) and workspace files (`agents.files.list`)
- Snapshot refresh pattern: parallel `agents.list` / `sessions.list` / `models.list` / `channels.status` RPCs on connect
- 42 unit tests

**Supporting files**: .desktop, .metainfo.xml, SVG icon, CSS (orange accent + libadwaita theming), README with per-distro build instructions

## Known gaps

- **Security review needed** — device identity handling, TLS verification flow, token persistence
- No packaging yet (Flatpak/deb/AppImage) — build-from-source only
- Tool-call rendering is basic (JSON in collapsible blocks)
- No system tray on tiling WMs (StatusNotifierWatcher absent) — fails gracefully
- Config editor is read-only
- Polling timers could be replaced with event-driven updates

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release` — zero warnings
- [x] `cargo test --release` — 66 tests pass (24 gateway-client + 42 app)
- [x] Smoke-tested against gateway v2026.4.3 on Arch Linux (bspwm/X11, Cairo renderer)
- [ ] Test on Wayland / GNOME Shell
- [ ] Test on Ubuntu 24.04 with stock GTK4/libadwaita packages
- [ ] Security audit of device identity + TLS flow

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(channels): recover stale ingress claims on startup
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #90945.

Telegram isolated ingress now stores spooled updates in the shared SQLite `channel_ingress_events` queue. If the gateway or handler dies after claiming a row, that durable row can stay `claimed` with a stale `claim_owner` and block later lane work. Existing recovery was queue-local and Telegram drain-time only, so a restart could leave old claimed rows invisible to normal pending drains.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds a shared startup recovery path for stale channel ingress claims instead of a Telegram-specific workaround. On gateway startup, old `claimed` rows are token-guarded back to `pending` across all channel ingress queues, with attempts/error metadata updated so the next drain can process them normally.

The startup sweep is best-effort and runs before channel startup maintenance. It deliberately uses age plus the exact stored claim token, not PID liveness, so PID reuse after a restart cannot keep an old claim protected forever and a freshly re-claimed row is not overwritten.

## User Impact

Users should no longer need manual SQLite edits after a crash/restart leaves Telegram ingress rows stuck as `claimed`. Messages accepted into OpenClaw state can become deliverable again on gateway startup instead of staying blocked behind a stale durable claim.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/message/ingress-queue.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-ingress-sweep.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/channels/message/ingress-queue.ts src/channels/message/ingress-queue.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-ingress-sweep.ts src/gateway/server-startup-ingress-sweep.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.ts src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/channels/message/ingress-queue.ts src/channels/message/ingress-queue.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-ingress-sweep.ts src/gateway/server-startup-ingress-sweep.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.ts src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

commands.author: Pandah97

target.title: fix(cron): detect recovered tool warnings without preferFinalAssistantVisibleText
target.body:
## Summary

An isolated scheduled `agentTurn` that recovers from an early tool error,
produces final assistant output, and ends with `status=success` should still
be eligible for delivery dispatch. Instead, `hasRecoveredToolWarning` in
`resolveCronPayloadOutcome` blocked recovery and `shouldUseFinalAssistantVisibleText`
required `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText === true`, which caused channels
like Feishu (where this flag is false) to classify the recovered run as having
a fatal structured error payload, causing `finalizeCronRun` to return before
`dispatchCronDelivery` was reached.

**Fix**: Two changes to `src/cron/isolated-agent/helpers.ts`:

1. Removed the `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText === true` requirement from
   `hasRecoveredToolWarning` so recovery detection depends on agent behavior
   (final output + all errors are tool warnings), not on channel preference.
2. Added `|| hasRecoveredToolWarning` to `shouldUseFinalAssistantVisibleText`
   so that recovered runs on non-preference channels still deliver the final
   assistant text instead of the stale warning payload.

Fixes #94846

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: An isolated cron agent that hits a temporary
tool warning (e.g. `⚠️ 🛠️ get_weather: API rate limited`) but then recovers
and produces valid final output was incorrectly classified as having a fatal
error on channels where `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText` is `false` (e.g.
Feishu). This caused `finalizeCronRun` to skip delivery dispatch entirely.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1,
OpenClaw commit 4d019f7c39.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx /tmp/proof-94849-v2.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #94849 (v2) ===
Runtime: v24.13.1 on linux x64

--- Test 1: Feishu-like channel (preferFinalAssistantVisibleText=false) ---
hasFatalErrorPayload: false
hasFatalStructuredErrorPayload: false
summary: The weather in NYC is sunny, 72°F.
outputText: The weather in NYC is sunny, 72°F.
deliveryPayloads: [{"text":"The weather in NYC is sunny, 72°F."}]

--- Test 2: Telegram-like channel (preferFinalAssistantVisibleText=true) ---
hasFatalErrorPayload: false
summary: The weather in NYC is sunny, 72°F.

--- Test 3: Tool warning without final output (still fatal) ---
hasFatalErrorPayload: true

--- Test 4: Non-tool error with final output (remains fatal) ---
hasFatalErrorPayload: true

=== Verification Summary ===
Feishu recovery detection:          PASS
Telegram no regression:             PASS
No-output still fatal:              PASS
Non-tool error still fatal:         PASS
All checks:                         PASS
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `resolveCronPayloadOutcome` correctly
detects recovered tool warnings regardless of `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText`.
Feishu-like channels now receive delivery dispatch with the final assistant
text, while non-tool errors and runs without final output remain correctly
classified as fatal. All existing Telegram-like behavior is preserved.

**What was not tested**: Live Feishu channel delivery. The proof validates
the `resolveCronPayloadOutcome` function at the runtime contract level using
the same source that production channels use.

## Tests and validation

- Updated the `detects recovered tool warnings without preferFinalAssistantVisibleText`
  test to verify `outputText` and `deliveryPayloads` are correctly set
- All 29 `resolveCronPayloadOutcome` tests pass unchanged
- 11 related cron run tests (payload-fallbacks + meta-error-status) pass

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)
`Yes` — Feishu (and other channels without `preferFinalAssistantVisibleText`)
will now receive delivery for recovered tool-warning runs, instead of silently
skipping delivery.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)
`No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)
`No`

What is the highest-risk area?
- Runs where all error payloads are tool warnings (`⚠️ 🛠️` prefix) but
  were previously intentionally treated as fatal due to channel preference
  — now they will proceed to delivery dispatch.

How is that risk mitigated?
- `isCronToolWarning` only matches the explicit `⚠️ 🛠️` prefix, so
  non-tool errors (other prefixes, plain text) remain unaffected
- `shouldUseFinalAssistantVisibleText` still independently respects
  channel preference for which text goes into the delivery payload
- The recovery guard (`normalizedFinalAssistantVisibleText !== undefined`)
  ensures the agent actually produced final output before marking run as recovered
- All existing tests pass unchanged

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

Attach at least one:

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: fix(agents): hint when unknown tools are skills
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem:** When a model mistakenly calls a skill name as a tool (e.g. `my-skill`), the error message is a generic "Tool not found" with no guidance, so the model keeps retrying or gives up. This is especially common with weaker/smaller models that struggle to distinguish skills from tools in the system prompt. - **Why it matters:** Skills are invoked differently from tools; without a hint the model has no way to self-correct, leading to repeated failed tool calls that waste tokens and frustrate users — particularly on cost-sensitive or lower-capability model configurations. - **What changed:** Register an `afterToolCall` hook in `attempt.ts` that intercepts error tool results, detects unknown-tool errors matching a registered skill name, and rewrites the message to `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` Also extracted `resolveUnknownToolName` helper in `errors.ts` (was inline regex) and exported `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` for the hook to call. - **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** `formatAssistantErrorText` signature and all its callers are untouched. No new params threaded through `run.ts`, `payloads.ts`, `subscribe.types.ts`, or lifecycle handlers. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Skills / tool execution ## Linked Issue/PR None ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - When a model calls a skill name as a tool and the skill is registered, the error tool result now says: `Tool "X" not found. "X" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` - When the tool name does not match any registered skill, behavior is unchanged (generic "Tool not found"). ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (Darwin 25.3.0, arm64) - Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm - Model/provider: Claude (Anthropic) - Relevant config: workspace with registered skills (e.g. `my-skill`, `another-skill`) ### Steps 1. Start an OpenClaw agent session with skills registered 2. Prompt the model to call a skill name as if it were a tool (e.g. "use tool my-skill") 3. Observe the error tool result returned to the model ### Expected - Error message: `Tool "my-skill" not found. "my-skill" is a skill. Read its SKILL.md first.` ### Actual - Confirmed: skill-matching errors get the hint; non-skill unknown tools get the generic error. ## Evidence - [x] Trace/log snippets Manually tested two scenarios: 1. Called skill names (`my-skill`, `another-skill`) as tools -> got skill hint message 2. Called nonexistent name (`banana123`) as tool -> got generic "Tool not found" ## Human Verification (required) - **Verified scenarios:** Both skill-match and non-match unknown tool errors in a live OpenClaw agent session. - **Edge cases checked:** Tool name that does not match any skill; empty skill snapshot; multiple registered skills. - **What you did not verify:** Behavior with the upstream pi-agent-core `immediate` branch change (afterToolCall on prepareToolCall); that path is covered by the pi-agent-core MR. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert commit `e527e3c111`; the `afterToolCall` hook is additive and removing it restores default unknown-tool errors. - Files/config to restore: `errors.ts`, `attempt.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If `setAfterToolCall` is called before the agent session is fully initialized, the hook may not fire; watch for missing skill hints in logs. ## Risks and Mitigations - **Risk:** `rewriteToolResultUnknownToolError` regex may not match all provider error formats. - **Mitigation:** Uses the same regex patterns already proven in `formatAssistantErrorText`; additional patterns can be added to `resolveUnknownToolName` without changing the hook wiring.

target.title: feat(session): add chat-channel session switching with LRU history retention
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #9959
- Related #28168

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added `/sessions` to list switchable sessions for the current chat SessionKey.
- Added `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for in-chat session switching.
- Current session is included in `/sessions`, marked as `[current]`.
- `/sessions` now shows compact single-line preview rows with session ID prefix and truncated preview text.
- Session switching now restores stored token/context/compaction snapshots for the target session.
- `session.historyLimit` now consistently supports `0` as “disable switchable session history”.
- Transcripts for sessions still in history remain switchable; transcripts are archived when evicted from the LRU history queue.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw dev runtime / source tree
- Model/provider: N/A for core session-switch logic
- Integration/channel (if any): chat-channel command flow; manually validated in a real chat session
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `session.historyLimit: 5` (default path)
  - `session.historyLimit: 0` (disable-history regression path)

### Steps

1. Start a chat conversation and create multiple sessions with `/new`.
2. Run `/sessions`, then switch via `/session <number>` or `/session back`.
3. Continue creating sessions until the history limit is exceeded.

### Expected

- `/sessions` lists current + recent switchable sessions in LRU order.
- Switching restores the target session’s stored metadata/snapshots.
- Sessions still present in history remain switchable.
- The oldest session is archived only after LRU eviction.
- With `session.historyLimit=0`, switchable history is disabled and the previous session is archived immediately on reset.

### Actual

- Behavior matches the expected results above in targeted tests and manual validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `/sessions` output in chat
  - `/session back` restores the previous session
  - switching to a still-retained historical session restores expected context/state
  - LRU eviction archives only the oldest evicted session
  - daily reset path preserves switchable history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` disables switchable history and archives immediately on reset
- Edge cases checked:
  - history boundary behavior at the configured limit
  - selecting current session is a no-op
  - unique-prefix SessionId selection behavior
  - stale/daily-reset session rollover
- What you did **not** verify:
  - every individual chat provider/plugin surface
  - cross-SessionKey switching (out of scope)
  - any UI/card-based session picker (out of scope)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - revert this PR, or set `session.historyLimit=0` to disable switchable session history
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
  - `src/config/types.base.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `/sessions` shows stale/incorrect ordering
  - switched sessions lose expected token/context/compaction snapshot state
  - transcripts are archived too early while still present in history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` does not disable switchable history

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: LRU eviction/archive timing could regress and archive a session that should still remain switchable.
  - Mitigation: Added/updated targeted tests covering retained-history vs evicted-history behavior.

- Risk: Snapshot restore could leave token/context/compaction state inconsistent after switching.
  - Mitigation: Added lifecycle tests covering restore and missing-metadata fallback behavior.

- Risk: Docs could drift from implementation again as follow-up fixes land.
  - Mitigation: Updated `docs/design/chat-session-switch.md` to match the current implementation semantics in this PR.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: Yes

I used an AI assistant for code review, doc cleanup, and targeted test updates. I manually reviewed the final diff, understood the changes, and verified the session-switch flows myself.

target.title: fix(google): add google-gemini-cli auth alias and expand model runtime bindings
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

After re-onboarding from another provider (e.g. Claude CLI) to Gemini CLI via `openclaw onboard`, the chat becomes unresponsive — the UI shows infinite loading and never returns a response. The onboarding completed successfully (auth tokens stored, config written), but sending a message hangs indefinitely.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Adds `"providerAuthAliases": { "google-gemini-cli": "google" }` to the Google extension manifest so `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli")` returns `"google"`. Without this alias, the auth profile fallback path in `resolveCliRuntimeFromAuthProfile()` cannot match `google-gemini-cli` profiles to the `google` provider, returning `undefined` and falling through to a direct Google API call without valid credentials.
- Expands the Gemini CLI config patch from a single model (`google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`) to all 7 active Gemini chat models. When users pick a model not in the original patch, the `agentRuntime` binding is absent from the `models` map, forcing the broken auth profile fallback path.
- Keeps existing Gemini CLI onboarding for first-time users and direct Google API key auth unchanged.

## User Impact

Users switching from another provider to Gemini CLI via `openclaw onboard` now get working chat responses instead of infinite loading. New Gemini CLI setups and existing Claude CLI / Google API key configs are unaffected.

## Evidence

- Branch base checked during freshness gate: `origin/main` at `ad3b2f4b88`. No final rebase was performed.
- Real behavior proof:
  - **Behavior or issue addressed:** `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider` returns `"google-gemini-cli"` for Google models after Gemini CLI onboarding, and `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli")` resolves to `"google"`.
  - **Real environment tested:** Linux Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw `main` branch, branch `fix/gemini-cli-auth-alias-reonboard`.
  - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
    ```bash
    pnpm test extensions/google/manifest.test.ts extensions/google/index.test.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --run
    ```
  - **Evidence after fix:**
    ```text
    Test Files  5 passed (5)
         Tests  80 passed (80)

    New test cases:
    - model-runtime-aliases.test.ts: resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider returns "google-gemini-cli"
      when model config binds it to a Google model
    - provider-auth-aliases.test.ts: resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli") returns "google"
      via the manifest providerAuthAliases
    - manifest.test.ts: Google manifest declares providerAuthAliases: { "google-gemini-cli": "google" }
    ```
  - **Observed result after fix:** `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider({ provider: "google", modelId: "gemini-3.1-pro-preview", cfg: { agents: { defaults: { models: { "google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview": { agentRuntime: { id: "google-gemini-cli" } } } } } } })` returns `"google-gemini-cli"` — the correct CLI execution provider. `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli", { metadataSnapshot })` returns `"google"`.
  - **What was not tested:** Live Gemini CLI OAuth onboarding with a real Google account sending a chat message end-to-end. The reproduction exercises the production `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider` and `resolveProviderIdForAuth` functions with real config shapes, but the full OAuth flow and Gemini CLI process execution are not exercised.
- Regression tests and local checks:
  ```text
  pnpm test extensions/google/manifest.test.ts extensions/google/index.test.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --run
  Test Files  5 passed (5)
  Tests  80 passed (80)

  git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
  passed with no output
  ```

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit tests
- Target test files: `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts`, `src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts`, `extensions/google/manifest.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider` resolves `"google-gemini-cli"` when model config binds it for a Google model
  - `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli")` returns `"google"` via the manifest `providerAuthAliases`
  - Google manifest declares `providerAuthAliases: { "google-gemini-cli": "google" }`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bugs are in (1) the manifest metadata declaration and (2) the config patch shape generated during onboarding. The new tests directly verify both points at the level where the bug lived.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Two interacting gaps. (1) The Google extension manifest had no `providerAuthAliases` entry mapping `google-gemini-cli` → `google`, so `resolveCliRuntimeFromAuthProfile()` could not recognize `google-gemini-cli` auth profiles as compatible with the `google` provider — the fallback returned `undefined` and the runtime attempted a direct Google API call without valid credentials. (2) The Gemini CLI config patch only set `agentRuntime` for `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`, so any other Gemini model selected during model picking had no runtime binding and fell through to the broken auth profile fallback.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered Anthropic/Claude CLI runtime resolution but not the Gemini CLI `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider → resolveCliRuntimeFromAuthProfile → resolveProviderIdForAuth` path, and no test verified the config patch covered all active models.

target.title: Fix #96484: Detect Cloudflare Sandbox as container environment via cloudchamber cgroup
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The container environment detection in `src/infra/container-environment.ts` uses cgroup regex matching to detect Docker, Podman, Kubernetes, and LXC containers. Cloudflare Sandbox (Cloudchamber) containers expose a `cloudchamber` or `cloudchamber_v2` cgroup entry that was not matched, causing diagnostics and runtime behavior to misidentify the environment as non-container.

## Why This Change Was Made

Added `cloudchamber` to the cgroup regex pattern so Cloudflare Sandbox containers are correctly detected as container environments. Updated the JSDoc to list all supported container runtimes including Fly Machines and Cloudflare Sandbox.

## Evidence

- The `containerEnvironmentCache` mechanism (lazy, cached) correctly returns `true` for Cloudflare Sandbox environments
- Existing container detection tests pass
- Detection order unchanged: Fly Machines env vars → sentinel files → cgroup regex
- TypeScript compilation verified clean

target.title: fix(copilot-oauth): bound OAuth and API endpoint response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`fetchJson` in `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts` reads the GitHub Copilot
OAuth and API endpoint response bodies with unbounded `await response.text()`
and `await response.json()`. A compromised or hijacked endpoint can stream an
arbitrarily large body and force the runtime to buffer the entire payload — an
OOM/DoS vector.

This is the same vulnerability class already hardened in the Anthropic OAuth
sibling (`src/llm/utils/oauth/anthropic.ts`, PR #96644).

## Changes

- `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts` — import `readResponseWithLimit`
  from `@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit`; replace both
  `await response.text()` and `await response.json()` with
  `readResponseWithLimit` at 16 MiB cap + `new TextDecoder().decode(buffer)` +
  `JSON.parse`. Uses `TextDecoder` to preserve UTF-8 BOM stripping semantics
  that `response.text()` provides.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

A real `node:http` server was started on `127.0.0.1`, and the real exported
`refreshGitHubCopilotToken` was driven against it over a real TCP socket
(global `fetch` stubbed only to rewrite the hostname to the loopback server).
Node v22.22.0.

```text
$ node --import tsx _proof_copilot_overflow.mts
[proof] cap=16777216 (16 MiB)

[test 1/3] oversized body — real refreshGitHubCopilotToken
[server] ← GET /copilot_internal/v2/token
  PASS  throws bounded cap error :: GitHub Copilot token refresh request response too large: 16794740 bytes
  PASS  client read 16794740 bytes (< cap + 1 MiB) :: clientRead=16794740, cap=16777216
[server] write ECANCELED after client stopped reading

[test 2/3] negative control — raw response.text() against oversized endpoint
[server] ← GET /copilot_internal/v2/token
  PASS  unbounded read: 24.0 MiB > 16 MiB cap :: buffered=25165824 bytes

[test 3/3] happy path — small valid JSON response
  PASS  happy path: parsed successfully :: access=pro-copilot-token
  PASS  happy path: access token is non-empty string :: access=pro-copilot-token
  PASS  happy path: expires is finite number :: expires=1799999700000

[proof] ALL PASS (pass=6 fail=0)
```

- **Oversized case**: `refreshGitHubCopilotToken` threw
  `GitHub Copilot token refresh request response too large: 16794740 bytes`;
  the server write was cancelled (`ECANCELED`) after the client stopped reading,
  confirming the stream was **cancelled**, not drained.
- **Negative control**: the OLD unbounded `response.text()` buffered the full
  **24 MiB** (≫ the 16 MiB cap), proving the cap is load-bearing.
- **Happy path**: small valid `{token, expires_at}` JSON parsed intact
  (`access=pro-copilot-token`).

### Tests

- **New Vitest case `rejects an oversized Copilot token refresh response`**
  creates a ReadableStream whose first chunk exceeds the 16 MiB cap and proves
  the bounded read cancels the body (`cancel` called once, `pull` count ≤ 2)
  and throws an error containing `"too large"`.
- **Existing 10 Vitest cases unchanged** — device flow, token refresh, model
  listing, timeout, and unsafe lifetime rejection all pass.
- `pnpm test src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.test.ts` → **11 passed (11)**

target.title: Fix #96525: Engage model fallback chain on cron result-level failures
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Isolated cron runs use `runWithModelFallback` for provider/model fallback, but the cron code path was missing the `classifyResult` and `mergeExhaustedResult` callbacks. Without these, the fallback chain could not detect result-level failures (reasoning-only, empty, or incomplete-turn results), causing cron runs to silently drop answers that a normal chat path would recover via fallback.

## Why This Change Was Made

Added `classifyEmbeddedAgentRunResultForModelFallback` as `classifyResult` and `mergeEmbeddedAgentRunResultForModelFallbackExhaustion` as `mergeExhaustedResult` to the cron `runWithModelFallback` call, matching the behavior already present in the normal chat path.

## Evidence

- The cron isolated-agent path now uses the same result-fallback classifier as the normal embedded agent runner
- TypeScript compilation verified clean

target.title: fix(session): percent-encode path separators in session dir to prevent cross-project collision
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`getDefaultSessionDir` encodes the cwd into a safe directory name by replacing path separators (`/`, `\`, `:`) all with `-`. This transform is non-injective: `/home/a/b-c` and `/home/a/b/c` both map to the same session directory. When `continueRecent` resumes the most recent session by mtime with no cwd verification, a user starting from `/home/a/b/c` can silently inherit `/home/a/b-c`'s conversation and tool-execution history.

## Why This Change Was Made

The encoding now uses URI percent-encoding (`%2F`, `%5C`, `%3A`) for path separators, making it injective. `continueRecent` also verifies the session header cwd matches the requested cwd as defense-in-depth.

## Evidence

- Two distinct cwds differing only by a path separator (`client/app` vs `client-app`) now produce different session directories
- `continueRecent` from one project starts a fresh session when the header cwd does not match the requested cwd
- TypeScript compilation verified clean

target.title: fix: streaming chat fails when gateway mislabels SSE as JSON [AI-assisted]
target.body:
> [AI-assisted] This PR was generated using Claude Code. Closes #96497.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users of self-hosted OpenAI-compatible gateways (vLLM, Ollama, custom proxies) would see every streaming chat turn fail before producing a reply with `Could not parse message into JSON: data: {...}` / `Unexpected token 'd', "data: {"id"... is not valid JSON` on OpenClaw 2026.6.x. The gateway returns standard single-prefix SSE (`data: {...}`), but when it mislabels the response `content-type` as `application/json`, OpenClaw re-wraps the body as `data: ${data}`, producing `data: data: {...}` which the OpenAI SDK cannot JSON.parse. This is a regression from 2026.5.4.

## Why This Change Was Made

In `sanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse` (`src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts`), the `synthesizeJsonAsSse` branch buffers a JSON-labeled streaming body and emits it as a single SSE event via `data: ${data}\n\n`. When the body is already valid SSE, that re-prefixes every frame to `data: data: {...}`.

The fix detects already-SSE bodies at the synthesis point using the existing `classifyOpenAISdkStreamBodyPrefix` classifier: if the buffered body classifies as `"sse"`, it is passed through verbatim (with a normalized single trailing event terminator) instead of being re-wrapped. Genuine JSON bodies (`{...}` / `[...]`) still take the original `data: ${data}` synthesis path, so non-SSE JSON streaming is unchanged. This directly implements the issue's requested tolerance for both `data: {...}` and `{...}` body shapes. Non-goal: it does not change content-type normalization for the `text/event-stream` path or the missing-content-type sniff path.

## User Impact

Streaming chat against OpenAI-compatible gateways that mislabel SSE as JSON now works again: the response streams normally instead of failing with a JSON parse error before the first token. Gateways that already send a correct `text/event-stream` content-type, and gateways that genuinely return JSON, are unaffected.

## Evidence

Regression repro added in `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts` ("does not double-prefix SSE bodies mislabeled as JSON by streaming gateways"). It drives the real `buildGuardedModelFetch` transport and the real OpenAI SDK `Stream.fromSSEResponse` parser against a response that serves real SSE frames (`data: {...}\n\n`) mislabeled with `content-type: application/json`.

Before the fix (parent commit), the repro fails with the exact error from the issue:

```
FAIL  src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts > buildGuardedModelFetch > does not double-prefix SSE bodies mislabeled as JSON by streaming gateways
SyntaxError: Unexpected token 'd', "data: {"id"... is not valid JSON
 ❯ Stream.iterator ../../node_modules/openai/src/core/streaming.ts:62:26
 Test Files  2 failed (2)
      Tests  2 failed | 146 skipped (148)
```

After the fix, the same repro streams correctly with no `data: data:` double-prefix:

```
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  2 passed | 146 skipped (148)

items = [
  { id: "a", choices: [{ index: 0, delta: { content: "Hi", role: "assistant" } }] },
  { id: "a", choices: [{ index: 0, delta: { content: " there" } }] },
]
```

Local verification on Windows, Node v22.22.3: `pnpm tsgo` clean; `oxlint` on changed files clean; changed files pass `format:check` (221 pre-existing repo-wide format findings on `main` are unrelated to this change). The `openai-responses`/non-`openai-completions` and genuine-JSON synthesis paths are covered by existing tests and remain green.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Streaming chat fails before reply when an OpenAI-compatible gateway returns real SSE mislabeled as JSON, because OpenClaw double-prefixes `data:` before JSON parsing.
- Environment used for testing: Windows 10, Node v22.22.3, OpenClaw main a0b397748f, real OpenAI SDK streaming parser.
- Steps to reproduce: Drive the real provider transport with a response serving `data: {...}\n\n` frames under `content-type: application/json`, then iterate the SDK SSE stream.
- Observed result before fix: SDK raises `SyntaxError: Unexpected token 'd', "data: {"id"... is not valid JSON`; no items produced.
- Observed result after fix: Two SSE frames parse into the expected delta items; no double-prefix.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture):
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.test.ts -t "double-prefix"
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  2 passed | 146 skipped (148)
   Duration  15.64s
```

## Compatibility / Migration

No config, schema, or public CLI surface changes. The new branch only activates for JSON-labeled bodies that are already SSE (previously broken); genuine JSON synthesis and `text/event-stream` sanitization are untouched. Revert by removing the `classifyOpenAISdkStreamBodyPrefix(data) === "sse"` branch in `sanitizeOpenAISdkSseResponse`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- A gateway that returns a body starting with an SSE field prefix (`data:`, `event:`, `:`, `id:`, `retry:`) but is not intended as SSE would now pass through verbatim instead of being wrapped. Such a body is already valid SSE by the SSE spec, so the SDK parses it the same way; genuine JSON (`{`/`[`) never matches the `"sse"` classification. Mitigated by reusing the existing classifier rather than introducing a new heuristic.

## What was not tested

- No live external gateway (vLLM/Ollama/cloud proxy) was available; the repro uses the real transport + real SDK parser against a local SSE response rather than a live provider call.
- macOS/iOS not tested; the fix is platform-agnostic transport-layer logic.
- `pnpm check:host-env-policy:swift` skipped on Windows (no Swift toolchain).

target.title: fix(agents): normalize bare date filenames to memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md in…
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #92372.

Multi-agent secondary agents with explicit workspaces fail to read daily memory files when the model drops the `memory/` prefix. The startup context prelude loads daily memory as `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`, guidance tells the model it's "already included," and the model forms a bare `YYYY-MM-DD.md` path — hitting ENOENT at the workspace root.

Additionally, the ENOENT retry branch synthesized `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` and called `params.base.execute` directly, completely bypassing the outer `wrapToolWorkspaceRootGuardWithOptions` sandbox validation. A symlinked `memory/` directory pointing outside the workspace would escape the sandbox (P1 security boundary).

## Why This Change Was Made

- Relax `DAILY_MEMORY_PATH_RE` to also match bare date filenames, then prepend `memory/` in `normalizeDailyMemoryReadPath`.
- Flatten `executeReadPage` into a two-phase flow: Phase 1 tries the original path; Phase 2 retries with the canonical `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` path only on ENOENT.
- Validate the synthesized retry path through `assertSandboxPath` (the same async guard used by the outer wrapper, including real-path symlink/hardlink/alias resolution via `assertNoPathAliasEscape`) before any filesystem access.
- Pass the workspace root via an optional `workspaceRoot` string — no callback indirection. Only injected when `workspaceOnly` is true.
- Preserve existing behavior: root-level date files succeed on first attempt, `memory/`-prefixed paths are unchanged, non-date filenames are unaffected, and `missingDailyMemoryReadResult` soft-fail is preserved.

## User Impact

Secondary agents with explicit workspaces can now successfully read daily memory files even when the model sends a bare date filename. No stale ENOENT errors, no wasted tokens. All synthesized fallback paths remain strictly confined inside the workspace sandbox — a symlinked `memory/` directory cannot escape.

## Evidence

Base checked during freshness gate: `origin/main` at `af2b0a6118`. One squashed commit on `fix/read-tool-bare-date-filename-memory-prefix`.

Regression tests first confirmed no regressions and the new tests fail on current `main`:

```
pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-paths.test.ts
# main: 15 passed; branch: 18 passed — 3 new tests exercise the bare-date redirect
# and symlink-escape guard, and would fail on main (bare date → permanent ENOENT,
# no symlink guard on retry path)
```

Validation after the fix:

- `pnpm tsgo` — passed clean
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts` — 28 tests passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts` — tests passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-paths.test.ts` — 18 tests passed (15 existing + 3 new)
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts` — tests passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-edit-access.test.ts` — tests passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.read.arg-value-suffix.test.ts` — tests passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts` — tests passed

Path normalization table:

| Input | Output | Status |
|---|---|---|
| `memory/2026-06-12.md` | `memory/2026-06-12.md` | unchanged |
| `2026-06-12.md` | `memory/2026-06-12.md` | corrected |
| `./2026-06-12.md` | `memory/2026-06-12.md` | corrected |
| `regular-file.md` | `undefined` | not triggered |

Security boundary verification:

| Scenario | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `memory/` is a regular directory in workspace | `assertSandboxPath` passes, retry proceeds |
| `memory/` is a symlink inside workspace | `assertSandboxPath` passes, retry proceeds |
| `memory/` is a symlink outside workspace | `assertSandboxPath` throws, no FS access |
| `workspaceOnly` is false | `workspaceRoot` is `undefined`, no validation |

## Reviewer Focus

- `agent-tools.read.ts:76` — relaxed regex
- `agent-tools.read.ts:255-269` — `normalizeDailyMemoryReadPath` with `memory/` prepend
- `agent-tools.read.ts:283-345` — flattened `executeReadPage` with Phase 1/Phase 2 + `assertSandboxPath` guard
- `agent-tools.ts:772` — `workspaceRoot` injection gated on `workspaceOnly`
- `agent-tools.workspace-paths.test.ts` — 3 new regression tests at end of `workspace path resolution` describe block

Files changed: `src/agents/agent-tools.read.ts`, `src/agents/agent-tools.ts`, `src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-paths.test.ts` (+139/-8 lines).

target.title: fix(backup): handle ENOENT race when session files are deleted during archive creation (fixes #67417) (AI-assisted)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`openclaw backup create` fails with ENOENT when a session file is cleaned up by the gateway's session compaction process between the backup scan phase and the tar archive phase. The entire backup fails instead of gracefully skipping the deleted file.

## Why This Change Was Made

The backup command enumerates all files in the state directory, then passes them to `tar.c()` for archiving. If the gateway's compaction process deletes a session `.jsonl` file between enumeration and archiving, `node-tar` throws ENOENT on `lstat` and the backup aborts entirely.

The existing `writeTarArchiveWithRetry` already handles EOF race errors (files changing size during archive). ENOENT from concurrent file deletion is the same class of race condition — a file that existed during discovery is gone when tar tries to read it.

The fix adds `isTarEnoentRaceError` to detect ENOENT errors with a `path` property (which `node-tar` sets when a file disappears mid-walk). These are retried with the same backoff strategy. On retry, `tar.c()` re-walks the directory tree; the deleted file no longer appears in the listing, so the archive succeeds.

## User Impact

- `openclaw backup create` no longer fails when session files are concurrently cleaned up
- The retry logs distinguish "concurrent file deletion" from "live-write race" for easier diagnosis
- The fix is backward-compatible: ENOENT errors without a `path` property (e.g. temp-dir cleanup) are still thrown immediately

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Evidence

- **Behavior addressed:** `openclaw backup create` fails with ENOENT when session files are deleted during archive creation
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node 22, OpenClaw 2026.5.28
- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. Built the project with `pnpm build`
  2. Ran `./node_modules/.bin/openclaw backup create --output /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test`
  3. Verified backup succeeds with volatile file skipping
  4. Ran targeted unit tests for the ENOENT retry logic
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ ./node_modules/.bin/openclaw backup create --output /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test
Backup skipped 1 volatile file (live sessions, cron logs, queues, sockets, pid/tmp).
Backup archive: /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test
Included 1 path:
- state: ~/.openclaw
Skipped 1 path:
- workspace: ~/.openclaw/workspace (covered by ~/.openclaw)
Created /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test
Skipped 1 volatile file (live sessions, cron logs, queues, sockets, pid/tmp).
```

Unit test results for the new ENOENT retry logic:

```
 ✓ matches ENOENT errors with a path property (node-tar stat race)
 ✓ rejects ENOENT errors without a path property
 ✓ rejects non-ENOENT errors even with a path
 ✓ rejects non-object inputs
 ✓ retries on ENOENT race errors from concurrent file deletion
 ✓ does not retry on ENOENT errors without a path property
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Backup completes successfully. ENOENT race errors are retried with backoff; on retry, the deleted file is absent from the directory listing and the archive succeeds.
- **What was not tested:** The actual concurrent deletion race condition (requires precise timing between gateway compaction and backup archiving). The retry logic is verified via unit tests with mock errors.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `openclaw backup create` fails with ENOENT when session files are deleted during archive creation
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node 22, OpenClaw 2026.5.28
- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. Built the project with `pnpm build`
  2. Ran `./node_modules/.bin/openclaw backup create --output /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test`
  3. Verified backup succeeds with volatile file skipping
  4. Ran targeted unit tests for the ENOENT retry logic
- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ ./node_modules/.bin/openclaw backup create --output /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test
Backup skipped 1 volatile file (live sessions, cron logs, queues, sockets, pid/tmp).
Backup archive: /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test
Included 1 path:
- state: ~/.openclaw
Skipped 1 path:
- workspace: ~/.openclaw/workspace (covered by ~/.openclaw)
Created /tmp/openclaw-backup-enoent-test
Skipped 1 volatile file (live sessions, cron logs, queues, sockets, pid/tmp).
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Backup completes successfully. ENOENT race errors are retried with backoff; on retry, the deleted file is absent from the directory listing and the archive succeeds.
- **What was not tested:** The actual concurrent deletion race condition (requires precise timing between gateway compaction and backup archiving). The retry logic is verified via unit tests with mock errors.
