target.title: fix(mattermost): record pending history for non-allowlisted group senders
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, messages from non-allowlisted senders are dropped at the access-check stage before `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` is called. The bot loses all conversation context from those users.
- **Why it matters:** Bots cannot summarize group conversations or answer questions about what non-allowlisted users said, even when an allowlisted user explicitly asks.
- **What changed:** Non-allowlisted messages now continue through the history-recording path (via a flag), then return without generating a response.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Access control behavior is unchanged — non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses. Only pending history recording is affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #57607
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** The `GROUP_POLICY_NOT_ALLOWLISTED` branch in the access-check block returns immediately, bypassing `recordPendingHistory()`. The mention-gating path correctly calls `recordPendingHistory()` before returning, but the allowlist path does not.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test verifies that pending history is recorded for dropped-but-visible group messages.
- **Prior context:** The access check and history recording were likely written at different times. The mention-gating path was added later with the correct pattern.
- **Why this regressed now:** This is not a regression — it has always been this way. The behavior becomes noticeable when users rely on `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` for access control while expecting the bot to maintain full group context.
- **If unknown, what was ruled out:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `monitor.channel-kind.test.ts` or `monitor.authz.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When a non-allowlisted sender posts in a group with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, the message should be recorded to `channelHistories` but no response should be generated.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A unit test on the monitor's message handling path can directly verify the history map is populated.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None found.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The existing test infrastructure for the monitor is complex. Adding a proper test requires familiarity with the test helpers and mocking patterns used in the project. Happy to add one with maintainer guidance.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Bots using `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` can now see messages from all group participants in their conversation context.
- Non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses (no change).
- When an allowlisted user @mentions the bot, the bot now has full group conversation context for better responses.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: NOT_ALLOWLISTED] -> [return] (no history recorded)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has no context] -> [cannot summarize]

After:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: set flag] -> [recordPendingHistory()] -> [return] (no response)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has full context] -> [accurate summary]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No` — messages were already received via WebSocket; this only affects whether they are stored in the in-memory pending history buffer.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 25.8.1
- Model/provider: openai/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel: Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "channels": {
      "mattermost": {
        "chatmode": "oncall",
        "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
        "groupAllowFrom": ["<user-id>"],
        "historyLimit": 50
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` and one user in `groupAllowFrom`.
2. Have a non-allowlisted user send messages in the group.
3. Have the allowlisted user @mention the bot asking to summarize or reference those messages.

### Expected

Bot responds with context from all group messages, including non-allowlisted users.

### Actual

Before fix: Bot has no context of non-allowlisted user messages.
After fix: Bot correctly includes non-allowlisted messages in its context.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets

Before: `mattermost: drop group sender=<id> (not in groupAllowFrom)` — no history recorded.
After: `mattermost: group sender=<id> not in groupAllowFrom (history-only)` followed by `mattermost: recorded history for blocked sender=<id>, not responding`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Tested with two Mattermost bot accounts, one allowlisted user, and multiple non-allowlisted users in a shared group channel. Confirmed bot can see and summarize non-allowlisted messages after patch.
- **Edge cases checked:** Verified non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger responses via @mention. Verified DM policy is unaffected. Verified other group policy modes (disabled, open) are unaffected.
- **What you did not verify:** Other channel plugins (Telegram, Signal, etc.) — this change only touches the Mattermost monitor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Increased memory usage from storing more history entries for high-traffic channels.
  - **Mitigation:** Bounded by the existing `historyLimit` setting (default 50). No unbounded growth.
- **Risk:** Non-allowlisted message content stored in memory could be seen by the bot in its responses.
  - **Mitigation:** This is the intended behavior — the bot already receives these messages via WebSocket. The change only affects whether they are available as context for future responses.

target.title: fix(xai): bound video response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

On the success path, the xAI video generation provider read the create and poll JSON responses with an unbounded `await response.json()`. The response body comes from an external, untrusted endpoint, so a hostile or buggy server that streams a very large body with no `Content-Length` could force the runtime to buffer the entire payload into memory before parsing it — an OOM / hang vector. This mirrors the sibling provider hardening landed in #96604–#96608 and #96875.

## Changes

- `extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.ts`: `readXaiVideoJson` (used by both the create and poll paths) now reads the body through the shared bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` (16 MiB cap) and parses the resulting bytes, instead of `await response.json()`. On overflow it throws `xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and cancels the stream; malformed/non-object payloads keep the existing `xAI video generation response malformed` behavior. The binary video download path already used `readResponseWithLimit` and is unchanged.
- `extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.test.ts`: existing mocks switched to real streamed `Response` bodies, plus two new regressions that drive an unbounded create/poll body and assert the bounded throw and stream cancellation.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The xAI video create and poll control JSON from an untrusted external endpoint must not be buffered whole on the success path. An oversized no-`Content-Length` body must stop at the 16 MiB cap, cancel the stream, and throw a bounded `...exceeds 16777216 bytes` error. Small valid JSON must continue to parse normally.
- **Real environment tested**: A real `node:http` server (`createServer`) bound to `127.0.0.1`, streaming JSON in 64 KiB chunks with no `Content-Length` and a would-stream size of ≈64 MiB. The script drove the provider's exact success-path wrapper `readXaiVideoJson` — reproduced byte-for-byte, calling the same exported bounded reader `readResponseWithLimit` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` with the identical 16 MiB cap and overflow error text — over a real loopback fetch + socket, for both the create and poll routes. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx scratchpad/xai-bound-proof.mts` — start the streaming server → call the create-path JSON reader and assert bounded throw + server-side socket abort near the cap → call the poll-path JSON reader and assert the same → run a negative control using old unbounded `fetch().arrayBuffer()` against the same streaming body → call the reader against a small valid JSON response.
- **Observed result after fix**: create JSON threw `xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes` and aborted after 19,464,207 bytes; poll JSON threw the same and aborted after 19,136,527 bytes. The negative control buffered the full 67,108,879 bytes, proving the cap is load-bearing. The small happy path parsed `request_id=req_happy status=done`. Focused Vitest: 15 passed.
- **What was not tested**: I did not hit the live xAI service; a live endpoint would not reproduce a hostile oversized body. Because the xAI video provider hard-codes `allowPrivateNetwork: false` and does not expose a per-request SSRF policy, the full exported provider cannot be driven over a loopback socket (the SSRF guard rejects 127.0.0.1); the TCP proof therefore drives the provider's exact success-path JSON wrapper and the real shared bounded reader over a real socket, and the create/poll provider wiring is covered by the focused in-repo Vitest regressions using the same reader and error text. In practice the control JSON is small; this is defense-in-depth. The proof script is not committed.

## Evidence

Real `node:http` terminal proof (real loopback socket, exact provider success-path wrapper, real shared bounded reader):

```
[proof] real node:http server on http://127.0.0.1:43519, cap=16777216 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes per overflow route
PASS  create JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  create JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=19464207 (<33554432)
PASS  poll JSON: overflow throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="xAI video generation response exceeds 16777216 bytes"
PASS  poll JSON: stream cancelled near 16MiB :: aborted=true bytesSent=19136527 (<33554432)
PASS  negative control: OLD unbounded read buffers PAST the 16MiB cap :: buffered=67108879 bytes (> 16777216)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded reads :: unbounded buffered=67108879 vs create sent≈19464207
PASS  happy path: small create JSON still parses :: request_id=req_happy status=done

[proof] 7 PASS / 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused Vitest:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/xai/video-generation-provider.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  15 passed (15)
```

Type checks:

```
pnpm tsgo:extensions        # exit 0
pnpm tsgo:extensions:test   # exit 0
```

**Label**: security

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(memory-host): add AbortSignal support to batch polling (OpenAI, Voyage, Gemini)
target.body:
Add AbortSignal support to batch polling loops to enable immediate cancellation when clients disconnect.

Problem
When HTTP clients disconnect during batch embedding operations, server-side polling continues until timeout, wasting API quota.

Solution
- Replace uncancelable setTimeout with cancellable version using clearTimeout
- Add signal?: AbortSignal parameter to all batch providers
- Use { once: true } to prevent listener accumulation (fixes MaxListenersExceededWarning)
- Thread signal through full call chain: batch-runner → runXxxBatches → waitForXxxBatch

Changes
- batch-runner.ts: Add signal to EmbeddingBatchExecutionParams type
- batch-openai.ts: Add signal support with {once: true} cleanup
- batch-voyage.ts: Update sleep type, add signal threading through deps
- batch-gemini.ts: Add signal support with {once: true} cleanup

Testing
- Verified polling stops within 1s of abort (was 30+ iterations)
- No listener leaks after long polls
- TypeScript compiles without errors

Backwards Compatible: signal is optional.

**Additional Fixes (Post-Review)**:
- Fixed `initial: batchInfo` parameter regression in `batch-voyage.ts` and `batch-gemini.ts` to avoid extra API calls on batch creation
- Fixed locale regression in `ui/src/ui/presenter.ts` (reverted hardcoded "en-US" to `undefined` for i18n compliance)

target.title: feat(plugins): add guard delivery and subagent review hooks
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the current hook surface can gate tool execution, but it cannot synchronously guard tool results before the agent sees them and it has no explicit pre-spawn / pre-return subagent review phases.
- Why it matters: phase 1-3 guard work needs a typed decision model plus real observation and subagent boundaries, otherwise "auto mode" and third-party policy plugins sit on incomplete seams.
- What changed: added shared guard decision/annotation types, new `before_tool_result_deliver` / `before_subagent_spawn` / `before_subagent_result_deliver` hooks, wired them into tool-result persistence and subagent spawn/announce flows, and extended targeted tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add a guard kernel, model-backed policy runtime, or any bundled automode/firewall plugin yet.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature

## Linked Issue/PR

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A

- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): N/A

- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/wired-hooks-subagent.test.ts`, `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.tool-result-persist-hook.test.ts`, `src/agents/sessions-spawn-hooks.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: guard decisions merge consistently, tool results can be warned/blocked before persistence, subagent spawn can be denied before launch, and child results can be rewritten before announce delivery.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests hit the exact hook seams without needing the full policy stack.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing hook wiring tests cover the older lifecycle hooks only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins can now return typed guard decisions and annotations from `before_tool_call`.
- Plugins can synchronously review and rewrite/block tool results before the next model turn via `before_tool_result_deliver`.
- Plugins can synchronously review/block subagent spawn and child-result delivery via `before_subagent_spawn` and `before_subagent_result_deliver`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees result]
[subagent spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [subagent announce]

After:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [before_tool_result_deliver] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees guarded result]
[subagent spawn] -> [before_subagent_spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [before_subagent_result_deliver] -> [subagent announce]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: new hook phases can block or rewrite execution/results, but they only extend existing plugin authority. Mitigation is typed decision shapes, synchronous ordering, and targeted seam coverage around the new hook paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default local repo config

### Steps

1. Register a plugin hook on `before_tool_result_deliver`, `before_subagent_spawn`, or `before_subagent_result_deliver`.
2. Run the corresponding tool/subagent flow.
3. Observe the result warning/block/rewrite before transcript persistence or subagent delivery.

### Expected

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve indentation when recombining non-streaming replies
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (OpenClaw agent-authored).
Testing: targeted local validation (gateway regression tests + targeted lint + pre-commit repo checks).

## Summary

- Companion to merged #55960, not a duplicate: #55960 fixed indentation loss while stripping inline reply directives in `src/utils/directive-tags.ts`; this PR fixes the remaining gateway-layer loss when non-streaming reply parts are recombined in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.
- Preserve each non-streaming payload chunk as authored when recombining it, so indentation-sensitive content survives payload boundaries.
- Reuse that same combiner for `/btw` side results so sibling non-streaming gateway paths behave consistently.
- Normalize `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` boundary trimming and add focused regression coverage for the single-newline, `/btw`, and CRLF cases.
- Scope boundary: no streaming-path formatting changes, no Markdown parsing changes, and no channel-specific formatting rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42576
- Related #29377
- Companion to merged #55960

## Relationship to #55960

Merged PR #55960 fixed one real whitespace-loss layer: inline reply-directive stripping in the shared parser (`src/utils/directive-tags.ts`).

This PR fixes a separate remaining layer later in the pipeline: gateway non-streaming recombination of final reply chunks (and `/btw` side-result chunks) in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.

So the two PRs are adjacent and complementary, not overlapping duplicates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-streaming final replies and `/btw` side results now preserve indentation when formatted content spans multiple payloads. When one chunk already ends with a single newline and the next chunk does not start with one, the gateway now preserves that authored single newline instead of forcing a blank line. The combiner also trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` cleanly at outer boundaries so Windows-style line endings do not leak stray carriage returns.

This fixes the flattened-output symptom reported against Matrix, but the bug itself is generic to gateway non-streaming recombination of indentation-sensitive content rather than Matrix-specific formatting.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: n/a (gateway-side reply recombination)
- Integration/channel (if any): reproduced against the gateway non-streaming `chat.send` path; user report was on Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): n/a

### Steps

1. Produce a non-streaming assistant reply whose formatted block is emitted across multiple final payloads.
2. Include indentation-sensitive content such as nested YAML, a nested list, or a fenced code block.
3. Observe the stored/broadcast final message text.

### Expected

- Indentation from each payload chunk is preserved in the final combined reply.

### Actual

- Leading whitespace at payload boundaries was trimmed away, flattening structured content.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Added focused regression coverage for:
- the authored single-newline separator case
- an indentation-sensitive `/btw` YAML side result
- `CRLF` boundary trimming

The linked user issue includes before/after screenshots of the real-world symptom.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted gateway regression test for the single-newline recombination edge case; targeted `/btw` side-result regression test for an indented YAML block; targeted `CRLF` boundary regression coverage; targeted lint on the changed files; repo pre-commit checks during commit.
- Edge cases checked: whitespace-only payload chunks are still ignored; separator insertion still only happens when adjacent chunks are not already separated by line breaks; authored single-newline separators are preserved instead of expanded to blank lines; `CRLF` boundaries do not leave stray carriage returns.
- What you did **not** verify: live Matrix delivery against a running instance, broader streaming reply behavior outside the existing `/btw` block-side-result coverage, or full-repo test suites.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commits `6b2461a11`, `c753cd0af`, and `d667f18f1` (or revert the PR branch changes as a unit)
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected extra blank lines between merged payload chunks, unexpected loss of an authored single newline between chunks, stray carriage returns from `CRLF` boundaries, or lost indentation in `/btw` side results

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving authored separators changes the old trim/join behavior for payloads that already end in a single newline.
  - Mitigation: this is intentional, explicitly regression-tested, and still bounded by the same whitespace-only-chunk skipping plus separator insertion only when neither side already provides a line break.
- Risk: preserving chunk text could expose `CRLF` edge cases at outer boundaries.
  - Mitigation: the combiner now trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` consistently at the start/end, with targeted regression coverage.
- Risk: reviewers may mistake this PR for a duplicate of #55960.
  - Mitigation: this description now names the exact layer split: `#55960` fixes directive parsing, this PR fixes gateway non-streaming recombination.

target.title: fix/agents-primary-model-selection-and-catalog-loading-41344
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes incorrect Primary Model selection when switching between agents in Agents > Overview. - Fixes partial / blank Primary Model dropdowns after refresh or first load by ensuring the model catalog is available on Agents Overview paths. - Adds targeted tests for both selection stability and catalog hydration behavior. - Keeps the fix scoped to the Agents Overview model-selection path. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #41344 - Related #46275 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: 1. The agent details subtree could reuse stale DOM/select state when switching agents, which caused the Primary Model select to drift toward the previously viewed agent's state. 2. Agents Overview depended on a shared model catalog that was not guaranteed to be loaded on refresh / first-load / overview-only paths, so the select could render before the catalog was ready and degrade into a partial or blank-looking list. - Missing detection / guardrail: - Existing coverage covered some selected-value behavior, but it did not cover model-catalog hydration timing or the refresh/overview path where the catalog starts empty and arrives later. - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - Prior work around #41344 / #46275 addressed the agent-switching selection issue. Revalidating that fix on latest `upstream/main` exposed a second, related lifecycle issue in Agents Overview model-catalog loading. - Why this regressed now: - The Overview UI depends on model-catalog data, but not all entry paths guaranteed that the catalog was loaded before rendering. - If unknown, what was ruled out: - Ruled out a pure selected-state-only explanation. The partial/blank dropdown after refresh was caused by catalog availability/lifecycle, not just DOM selection drift. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.browser.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - Switching between agents keeps the correct explicit Primary Model selected. - The inherit-default option text does not go blank. - When the model catalog is initially empty, Agents Overview shows a loading state instead of a misleading partial list. - When the catalog hydrates later, the full list appears without losing the selected value. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - The bug spans both UI render behavior and shared model-catalog lifecycle, so a combination of focused controller/unit tests plus browser-level select behavior tests is the smallest reliable coverage. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - Existing browser coverage covered some selection behavior, but not the catalog hydration path. - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Switching between agents no longer causes the Primary Model select to show the wrong agent's value. - On refresh / first entry to Agents Overview, the Primary Model select no longer degrades into a misleading partial list. - While the model catalog is still loading, the UI now shows a loading state instead of pretending only a small subset of options exists. - Once the catalog loads, the full option list appears without losing the current selection. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 10 - Runtime/container: local dev environment - Model/provider: local UI validation only - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): standard Agents UI configuration with default and non-default agents ### Steps 1. Open Agents > Overview > Model Selection > Primary Model. 2. Switch between a default agent and a non-default agent. 3. Refresh the page and reopen the Primary Model select immediately. 4. Optionally wait for model-catalog hydration or switch away/back to Overview. ### Expected - The select reflects the correct explicit value for the current agent. - The inherit-default option text is stable. - Refresh / first-load does not show a misleading partial catalog. - Once the catalog is available, the full model list appears without losing the selected value. ### Actual - Before this fix, switching agents could retain stale select state from another agent. - Before this fix, refresh / first-load could show only fallback options (for example inherit/current) or appear blank until the catalog became available later. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ### UI Evidence #### Before After refresh / first load, the Primary Model dropdown could show only fallback entries or appear blank. #### After After the fix, the Primary Model dropdown restores the full option list correctly after refresh / first load, without losing the selected value. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Switched repeatedly between default and non-default agents and confirmed Primary Model stays tied to the current agent. - Refreshed the page and reopened Agents Overview to verify the select no longer falls into a misleading partial/blank state. - Confirmed catalog hydration restores the full list without losing the selected value. - Edge cases checked: - Inherit-default text path - Explicit non-default model selection - Refresh / first-load / return-to-overview behavior - What you did **not** verify: - Full end-to-end coverage across unrelated panels/modules - Additional stale/error UX beyond this fix's loading-state behavior ## AI Assistance - This PR was developed with AI assistance (CodeX). - I reviewed the resulting changes, reproduced the bug locally, and verified the final behavior manually. - Testing level: fully tested locally with targeted automated tests and manual UI verification. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: - N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: - Revert this PR's two commits. - Files/config to restore: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents-panels-overview.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-render.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-settings.ts` - `ui/src/ui/app-chat.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: - Primary Model shows another agent's value after switching agents - Refresh / first-load shows only partial model options or a blank-looking select - Catalog hydration changes the list but drops the selected value ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: - Multiple UI entry points now ensure model-catalog availability, which could increase repeated refresh attempts if not properly deduped. - Mitigation: - The loading/ensure logic is centralized in the shared model controller and covered by targeted tests. - Risk: - Keyed subtree remounting can discard unsaved local UI state when switching agents. - Mitigation: - That tradeoff is intentional here because correctness of agent-specific model selection is more important than preserving stale subtree state across agent switches.

target.title: fix(ios): stabilize chat streaming layout and session flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: iOS chat streaming could attach to a synthetic placeholder lane, churn during initial history/bootstrap, and reparse streaming content in ways that caused flicker or session drift.
- Why it matters: active conversations can show unstable streaming UI, lose the correct lane/session association, or visibly flicker while tokens arrive.
- What changed: scoped streaming to the active session key, hardened stream acceptance/initial load behavior, and stabilized layout parsing/visibility updates.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no new model behavior. This fix lives in the shared `OpenClawChatUI` layer used by both iOS and macOS. The bug (cross-run UI drift during concurrent session-key agent events) affects any client using `ChatViewModel`; macOS uses the same chat view and benefits equally, but the fix is not platform-specific — it guards shared streaming state.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: targeted shared-package tests plus iOS build validation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40985
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: streaming state was not consistently tied to the active session key, and downstream UI/layout updates re-parsed or re-evaluated visibility in ways that amplified bootstrap churn into visible instability.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was insufficient coverage around session-scoped streaming updates plus layout stability under rapid token updates/history refresh.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): this branch supersedes #40985 by fixing the broader lane/acceptance/layout causes instead of only the earlier visible symptom.
- Why this regressed now: incremental fixes addressed isolated symptoms while the newer streaming lane introduced more state transitions that needed session-scoped and layout-stable handling.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: the issue was not isolated to one text-rendering widget; it involved session scoping, bootstrap timing, and layout parsing together.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/AssistantTextParserTests.swift`
- Scenario the test should lock in: session-scoped streaming updates must survive bootstrap/history churn and preserve stable parsed output/visibility while tokens stream.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared chat state/parsing logic, so package-level tests cover the core behavior without requiring a full end-to-end harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): targeted parser/view-model tests were added/expanded for this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- iOS chat streaming stays attached to the active session instead of a synthetic placeholder lane.
- Streaming text/layout behaves more stably during initial load/history refresh.
- Visible flicker/churn from repeated parse/visibility mismatches is reduced.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local dev environment
- Runtime/container: shared OpenClawKit package + iOS app build
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): iOS chat UI / streaming flow
- Relevant config (redacted): local iOS simulator build

### Steps

1. Build the shared package and iOS app from this branch.
2. Open a chat session and trigger a streaming reply.
3. Observe initial history/bootstrap plus active token streaming.

### Expected

- Streaming binds to the active session.
- Bootstrap/history churn does not destabilize the active stream.
- Layout remains visually stable while tokens arrive.

### Actual

- Matched expected in targeted tests and local build validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests`
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests`

  - stream acceptance during bootstrap/history load

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: chat-state timing bugs can reappear under bootstrapping or external-final sync edge cases.
  - Mitigation: this patch scopes state to the active session and adds targeted parser/view-model coverage around the churn paths.



## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: iOS/shared OpenClawChatUI streaming state remains scoped to the active session, load recovery remains idempotent after a failed initial bootstrap plus successful manual refresh, and streaming text/tool state does not get cleared by a later view `load()` call.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local dev environment, Xcode 26.5 SDKs, Swift Testing 1902, OpenClawKit shared package and generated iOS Xcode project from this PR head `591a724df2531d373fc8603f2faa323fc93bc053`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml
xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClawChatUI -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
ChatViewModelTests: Test run with 83 tests in 1 suite passed after 5.550 seconds.
AssistantTextParserTests: Test run with 7 tests in 1 suite passed after 0.001 seconds.
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml: Created project at /Users/eulices/projects/openclaw-pr50483/apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj
xcodebuild OpenClawChatUI generic iOS Simulator: ** BUILD SUCCEEDED **
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The recovered-load regression is covered by `successful refresh after failed load makes later load idempotent`, which passed after confirming a failed first bootstrap can recover through manual refresh and a later `load()` does not issue another history request or clear the active stream. The shared `OpenClawChatUI` target also built successfully for iOS Simulator from the generated project.
- **What was not tested**: Full `OpenClaw` app scheme build was attempted but blocked by local Xcode environment missing watchOS 26.5 for the embedded Watch app; the directly changed shared chat UI target was built instead. Full repository CI matrix was left to GitHub.

target.body: ## Summary > This is the follow up PR of #46279 > It was heavily written with the help of Gemini 3.1 Pro (mainly for building UI) and Codex GPT 5.4, including lots of review iterations. > During the implementation of the previous PR I moved more and more logic into a shared layer, which caused lots of review comments about code I didn't write. I tried to let Codex write regression tests for several of these cases to ensure that I don't break features. > Manual testing with my Pixel Watch 2 and Pixel 8 Pro was part of the whole process. Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: OpenClaw did not have a Wear OS companion. - Why it matters: The watch needs a compact, reliable chat surface with fast reply actions, honest connection state, and behavior that still works on tiny round screens and when the app is backgrounded. - What changed: Added a new Wear OS app module, phone-side Wear proxy service for Tailscale support, direct watch gateway client, transcript-first chat UI, session picker, split settings screens, tile + complication support, background reply notifications with quick reply. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change gateway orchestration semantics, tools/skills behavior, desktop/iOS/macOS clients, or the Android phone app's broader UI theme beyond the phone-side Wear proxy integration. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # (none) - Related # (none) ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - New Wear OS companion app with a transcript-first chat flow and a two-page swipe layout. - Chat opens on the transcript page and follows the latest message by default; the primary edge action switches between `Reply` and `Latest`. - Added session picker, chat settings, connection settings, reconnect flow, and direct-vs-phone-proxy connection support on the watch. - Added Wear tile and complication entry points. - Added native watch notifications for background replies, including quick reply via text/voice dictation. - Added optional TTS playback and configurable screen-awake behavior on the watch. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`Yes`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`Yes`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The Wear app adds `POST_NOTIFICATIONS`, Wear Data Layer messaging, direct gateway WebSocket access, and storage of watch-side gateway config including token-based direct auth. Risk is constrained by using the existing authenticated gateway paths, limiting watch data access to the same session/gateway calls already exposed by the phone client, honoring platform notification permission, and keeping watch auth/config local to the Wear app. Phone-proxy traffic stays within the paired phone/watch channel and only proxies requests through the phone's active operator session. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Android phone + Wear OS 3/4 watches; manually tested on Pixel Watch 2 and Wear round emulators (small + large) - Runtime/container: Android Studio/Gradle debug builds - Model/provider: Existing OpenClaw gateway via phone proxy and direct gateway modes - Integration/channel (if any): Wear Data Layer between paired phone and watch - Relevant config (redacted): Matching debug-signed phone + watch builds; direct mode host/port/token/TLS when used; phone-proxy mode with phone app installed and signed the same way ### Steps 1. Build and install matching debug phone and Wear builds, then launch the Wear app on a paired watch. 2. Verify transcript flow, reply actions, session switching, settings, direct mode, phone-proxy mode, tile/complication entry points, and reopen/background behavior. 3. Send assistant replies while the watch app is foregrounded and backgrounded, then verify reply notifications, quick reply, reconnect/disconnect behavior, and latest-message scrolling. ### Expected - The watch should show a compact, reliable chat UX with correct connection state, preserved transcript state, working reply paths, and background reply notifications. ### Actual - Matches expected in manual testing on Pixel Watch 2 and both small/large Wear emulators. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Actual screenshots from Pixel Watch 2: | | | | |---|---|---| | Empty tanscript view | Empty transcript view | Swipe over to menu | | Scroll down in menu | Scroll further down | Click behavior settings button | | Scroll down | Click screen awake setting | Click screen awake setting again | | Go back and enter connection settings | Scroll down to helper text | Disable phone proxy | | Scroll down to gateway config | Scroll further down | Go back to transcript view | | Scroll down to see reply edge button (*default view* if you open the app≈) | Reply button toggles voice input (or let's you pick first, if disabled in settings) | Thinking animation | | When closing app, notification is delivered| Tile adding dialog with preview of tile | Actual tile | | Watch face with complication at top and bottom right (different formats) | ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Manually tested on a Pixel Watch 2 via Tailscale phone proxy and in both small and large round Wear emulators. Verified chat launch, latest-message behavior, reply flow, session picker, chat settings, connection settings, direct gateway mode, phone-proxy mode, tile launch, complication launch, background reply notifications, and quick reply from the notification. - Edge cases checked: Reopening the app after backgrounding, disconnect/reconnect flow, switching away from and back to the transcript page, scrolling away from latest and jumping back, and testing on both small and large round watch layouts. - What you did **not** verify: every possible Android OEM pairing/background-policy variant, and exhaustive real-network failure cases beyond the implemented reconnect/error handling paths. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: None. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the Wear branch commits or ship only the phone build without installing the Wear module; on-device, switch the watch back to phone-proxy or disconnect direct mode if direct gateway behavior is problematic. - Files/config to restore: `apps/android/wear/**`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/wear/WearProxyService.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/java/ai/openclaw/app/NodeRuntime.kt`, `apps/android/app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml`, and `apps/android/app/build.gradle.kts`. - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: watch stuck showing connected when the proxy/direct gateway is not actually usable, sent messages disappearing after history refresh, explicit disconnect reconnecting by itself, reply notification not surfacing or lacking quick reply, and transcript reopening at the wrong position. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Phone-proxy mode depends on both Wear Data Layer reachability and the phone app having a live operator session. - Mitigation: The watch exposes direct mode as an alternative, surfaces connection state in the UI, and includes reconnect/disconnect controls. - Risk: The second page is dense on the smallest round watches, especially with long session names/keys. - Mitigation: Manual testing covered both small and large round emulator sizes and a real Pixel Watch 2. - Risk: Background reply notifications depend on platform permission and Wear notification presentation. - Mitigation: The app requests notification permission explicitly, and manual verification covered notification delivery plus quick reply behavior.

target.title: fix: drain inbound debounce buffer and followup queues before SIGUSR1 reload
target.body:
## Problem

When `config.patch` triggers a SIGUSR1 restart, two in-memory message buffers are silently wiped, causing inbound messages to be permanently lost:

1. **Inbound debounce buffers** — per-channel debounce timers (`createInboundDebouncer` closure-local `Map` + `setTimeout`) that batch messages over a ~2500ms window. On restart, the `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and buffered messages are garbage collected.

2. **Followup queues** — the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global `Map` that collects messages for sessions with active agent turns. Items enqueued but not yet drained are lost when the server reinitializes.

### Reproduction
Send a message on Signal → within 2.5s, trigger a config change (e.g. `config.patch`) → the message is silently dropped.

## Fix

### 1. Global debouncer registry (`src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`)
- Each `createInboundDebouncer()` now auto-registers in a shared `Symbol.for('openclaw.inboundDebouncers')` global map
- New `flushAll()` method on each debouncer instance that immediately processes all buffered items (clears timeouts, calls `onFlush` callbacks)
- New exported `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` that iterates the registry, flushes all debouncers, and deregisters them

### 2. Followup queue drain waiter (`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts`)
- New `waitForFollowupQueueDrain(timeoutMs)` that polls `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` until all queues finish processing (or timeout)
- Counts `draining: true` queues as having pending work even if `items.length` is momentarily 0

### 3. SIGUSR1 restart integration (`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts`)
Before `markGatewayDraining()`, the restart flow now:
1. `await flushAllInboundDebouncers()` — pushes buffered messages into followup queues
2. `await waitForFollowupQueueDrain(5000)` — gives the drain loops 5s to process newly flushed items
3. Then proceeds with the existing `markGatewayDraining()` → task drain → server close flow

The ordering is critical: flush debouncers first (triggers `onFlush` callbacks that enqueue into followup queues), then wait for followup drain, then mark draining.

## Tests

- **`src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` flushes multiple registered debouncers, returns count, deregisters after flush; `createInboundDebouncer.flushAll` flushes all buffered keys
- **`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts`**: immediate return when empty, waits for drain completion, returns not-drained on timeout, counts actively draining queues as pending
- **`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`**: SIGUSR1 restart calls flush before `markGatewayDraining`, skips followup wait when no debouncers had buffered messages, logs warning on followup drain timeout

All 118 tests pass across the 4 affected test files. No type errors introduced.

## Files Changed
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — global registry + `flushAll` + `flushAllInboundDebouncers()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts` — new `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` — hook into restart flow
- Test files for all of the above

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a SIGUSR1 restart fires within the inbound debounce window (~2.5s) or while a followup queue is mid-drain, buffered messages are silently dropped because the in-process `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and the closure-local `Map` is garbage collected. After this fix, `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` immediately flushes every registered debouncer into followup queues and `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()` blocks the SIGUSR1 path until those queues drain or a 5s ceiling is hit.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `5d1e6592d02782361b871581c10a6b3c0329105f` of branch `fix/drain-queue-before-sigusr1`. Linux 6.12.67, Node v24.15.0, pnpm-driven monorepo. Patched modules loaded directly with `tsx` from the working tree (no rebuild step), and the built CLI (`pnpm openclaw --version`) reports `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)` confirming the binary is built from the patched commit.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw --version` to confirm the live CLI is built from the patched HEAD.
  2. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303.mts` — small driver that registers two `createInboundDebouncer` instances with a 2500ms debounce, enqueues five items across three keys, then calls `flushAllInboundDebouncers({ timeoutMs: 5000 })` and prints the timing and what each `onFlush` actually received.
  3. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303-drain.mts` — driver that exercises `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` directly against the live `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` `Map` for three cases: empty registry, busy queue that drains at ~120ms, and a queue that stays busy past the timeout.
  4. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — full targeted suites for all three changed files.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from running the patched modules in this worktree.

  Live CLI build proves the binary contains the patched code:

  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)
  ```

  `flushAllInboundDebouncers` console output — 5 enqueued items across 2 debouncers and 3 keys, flushed in 1ms instead of waiting 2500ms:

  ```
  [before flush] A delivered=0, B delivered=0 (still buffered)
  [flushAllInboundDebouncers] flushedCount=3 elapsed=1ms (vs 2500ms debounce)
  [after flush] A delivered groups=[["alice:hello","alice:there"],["bob:howdy"]]
  [after flush] B delivered groups=[["ping-1","ping-2"]]
  ```

  `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` console output — three live cases against the real `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` map, including the timeout path that returns `drained:false` with the right `remaining` count:

  ```
  [empty]         {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=0ms
  [busy->drained] {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=151ms
  [timeout]       {"drained":false,"remaining":2} elapsed=200ms
  ```

  Real test runner output for the changed files (vitest reports the actual function counts; pnpm `test-projects.mjs` shards by project):

  ```
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  24 passed (24)
     Duration  2.21s
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  4.05s
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.96s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` deterministically drains buffered Signal-style messages in 1 ms while the natural debounce window is 2500 ms, and reports the right buffer count (`flushedCount=3` for two keys in one debouncer plus one in another). `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` returns immediately when the registry is empty, observes the queue clearing in real time during the busy case (151 ms — one poll interval past the 120 ms cleanup), and correctly returns `drained:false` with `remaining=2` when the timeout fires before drain. The CLI banner reports `(5d1e659)` confirming the built binary contains the patched commit. With the SIGUSR1 hook in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` calling these helpers in the right order before `markGatewayDraining()`, late inbound messages are pushed into followup queues and given up to 5 s to drain instead of being silently dropped.
- **What was not tested**: An end-to-end real-Signal SIGUSR1 reproduction was not executed on this machine (no signal-cli account paired here); the production restart path is exercised through `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`, but a live Signal → `config.patch` → restart roundtrip on real `signal-cli` was not run. Behavior under simultaneous restart-while-flushing on a hung `onFlush` (the registered handle is intentionally retained on flush failure for a future sweep) was not exercised live; that path is covered only by the unit tests in `src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`.

target.title: fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `event.messages` was documented as a generic user-reply channel, but only `/new` and `/reset` actually drained those messages.
- Why it matters: `message:received` hooks could push reply text that was silently dropped, while other lifecycle hooks looked supported even though they had no safe reply target.
- What changed: added a shared internal hook reply-drain helper, kept reset/new command behavior, and now deliver `message:received` hook replies when a routable reply target exists.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not make bootstrap, preprocessed/transcribed, sent, or other lifecycle-only hooks auto-reply.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39688
- Related #40185

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Internal hook replies pushed to `event.messages` are now delivered on `message:received` when OpenClaw has a reply-capable routing target.
- Docs now explicitly limit `event.messages` auto-delivery to reply-capable surfaces instead of implying it works for all hooks.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): internal hooks / inbound reply routing
- Relevant config (redacted): default hook + routeReply test harnesses

### Steps

1. Register an internal hook that pushes text into `event.messages`.
2. Trigger it from `/new` or `message:received`.
3. Verify the reply is routed only when a safe reply target exists.

### Expected

- Reply-capable surfaces route the accumulated hook messages.
- Lifecycle-only surfaces remain non-replying unless they gain an explicit delivery contract later.

### Actual

- Before this fix, `message:received` hook replies were silently dropped.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: `commands-core` still routes reset/new hook replies; `dispatch-from-config` now routes `message:received` hook replies on routable surfaces and skips non-routable ones.
- Edge cases checked: non-routable surfaces do not auto-send hook replies; docs/comments now match that contract.
- What you did **not** verify: live messaging-provider delivery outside the existing routeReply test harnesses.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert `fix(hooks): deliver internal hook replies on replyable surfaces`
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/internal-hook-replies.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-core.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`, `docs/automation/hooks.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate or unexpected hook replies on inbound turns, or `message:received` hook replies not routing when they should.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `message:received` hook replies use the same route-reply path as other provider-routed responses, so a bad target selection would misroute the hook message.
  - Mitigation: the helper uses existing inbound routing fields (`OriginatingTo`, `To`, channel normalization) and adds explicit tests for routable and non-routable surfaces.
- Risk: this branch also carries a whitespace-only `CONTRIBUTING.md` formatter cleanup required for `pnpm check`.
  - Mitigation: it is isolated in its own commit and does not affect runtime behavior.

target.title: Fix dashboard chat run recovery across reconnects
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes two closely related Gateway Dashboard chat reliability problems:

1. Dashboard chat could lose busy-state on reconnect/tab return and incorrectly fall back to `New session` / `Send` while the session still had an active run.
2. Gateway restarts could interrupt an in-flight chat run without leaving a visible assistant-side notice in the chat transcript.

## What changed

- include `activeChatRuns` in the gateway hello snapshot
- recover `chatRunId` / busy state from snapshot on reconnect instead of blindly clearing it
- keep the composer in busy mode while a run is still abortable
- stop forcing sidebar chat navigation back to `main`; prefer the last active chat session
- abort active chat runs during gateway shutdown and inject a durable assistant transcript notice
- process restart sentinel wake immediately on startup and inject a transcript notice for internal sessions without external delivery routes

## Validation

- `pnpm vitest run src/gateway/server-close.test.ts src/gateway/server-restart-sentinel.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=6144 pnpm -s tsc --noEmit --pretty false`

## Related

- Closes #41064
- Related: #38836
- Related: #21641
- Related: #26986

target.title: fix(pixverse): bound video-generation success JSON response reads at 16 MiB
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/pixverse/video-generation-provider.ts:189` calls `await response.json()` with no byte-level cap. A hostile, compromised, or misconfigured PixVerse endpoint can return a success status with no `Content-Length` and stream an arbitrarily large JSON payload, causing OOM.

The fix routes through `readProviderJsonResponse` (openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http, 16 MiB default cap), which reads the body as a bounded stream and cancels on overflow. The existing `readPixVerseSuccess` layer is preserved for PixVerse-specific envelope validation.

## Changes

- `extensions/pixverse/video-generation-provider.ts:186-190` — replace `payload = await response.json()` in `readPixVerseJson` with `readProviderJsonResponse(response, label)`. Removes the manual try/catch (malformed JSON handling is built into the SDK helper). Type changed from `Pick<Response, "json">` to `Response`.
- `extensions/pixverse/video-generation-provider.test.ts` — add `readProviderJsonResponseMock` to destructured mock imports (shared mock handles the delegation).
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/provider-http-mocks.ts` — add `readProviderJsonResponseMock` to shared mock interface and the `vi.mock("openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http")` block, so extension tests can mock it alongside other provider-http exports.

## Real behavior proof

Drives `readProviderJsonResponse` over a real `node:http` server (chunked transfer, no Content-Length). Node v22.22.0.

```
=== PixVerse bounded-read proof ===

  PASS  happy path: status ok
  PASS  happy path: returns object with Resp — video_id=123
  PASS  oversized: throws bounded error with label — err=PixVerse test: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes
  PASS  oversized: bytes on wire stayed near cap (< 20 MiB) — bytesSent=18.4 MiB, cap=16 MiB

=== PixVerse bounded-read: 4/4 passed ===
```

- **Behavior addressed**: unbounded `response.json()` on PixVerse video generation success responses; now capped at 16 MiB (SDK default) with cancellation on overflow.
- **Real environment tested**: real `node:http` server (127.0.0.1, chunked transfer, no Content-Length). Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx _proof_pixverse.mts` (working tree only, not committed)
- **Evidence after fix**: 4/4 assertions pass. Happy path: valid PixVerse envelope parsed correctly. Oversized (endless chunked stream): throws "JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes" at ~18.4 MiB.
- **What was not tested**: live PixVerse API call (the proof exercises the same `readProviderJsonResponse` helper used in production).

## Out of scope

- Other unbounded `response.json()` call sites in `extensions/pixverse/` — covered in separate bounded-read sibling PRs.
- Default cap policy — 16 MiB is the SDK helper default, matching all other non-image provider JSON reads.

## Risk

- **Low**: swap from `response.json()` to `readProviderJsonResponse`. Error behavior preserves the same upstream catch path. Byte cap is 16 MiB — invisible to normal PixVerse JSON responses (typically < 1 MiB).
- **Tested**: 15/15 existing unit tests pass.

## Diff stats

```
3 files changed, 10 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)
```

AI-assisted: implemented and proof-tested with AI assistance; reviewed by a human before submission.

target.title: fix(cron): add Authorization header to provider preflight probe
target.body:
## Summary

Replaced the hand-rolled Authorization header logic in the cron preflight probe with the shared `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` from `provider-request-config.ts`. This ensures the preflight and normal model request paths build identical auth headers for the same config.

## Changes

### P1: Reuse shared auth normalization (ClawSweeper finding)

- **model-preflight.runtime.ts**: Replaced the manual `if (params.apiKey) { headers["Authorization"] = ... }` / custom-header construction with delegation to `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` via `sanitizeConfiguredProviderRequest()`
- Auth is now resolved through the same code path as normal model requests — any future changes to auth formatting apply automatically
- Full credential chain preserved: `request.auth` override → `resolveApiKeyForProvider` fallback → no auth
- Also ensures `request.headers` are always passed through (even without `request.auth`), fixing a pre-existing gap where provider-level and request-level headers were silently dropped

### P0: Error redaction at cache-write time

- **model-preflight.runtime.ts**: Applied `formatErrorMessage()` to errors before storing in `preflightCache`, preventing unredacted error objects from remaining in heap between cache write and read

### P0: Header prefix trimming consistency

- Prefix is now trimmed via shared `sanitizeConfiguredProviderRequest`, matching the behavior of `resolveAuthOverride` in the normal request path

### P2: Cross-path auth header parity tests

- **model-preflight.runtime.test.ts**: Added 4 parity tests (P0) verifying preflight and normal path produce identical auth headers for the same config
- Added 4 edge case tests (P1): empty header fields, unknown mode, whitespace token, OAuth marker filtering
- Total: 31 tests pass

## Files Changed

| File | Δ |
|------|---|
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.ts` | +140/-40 |
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts` | +445/-3 |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron job preflight was building custom auth headers (`request.auth.mode === "header"`) with hand-rolled logic that missed prefix trimming, header collision cleanup, and `request.headers` merging — inconsistent with the shared auth normalization used by normal model requests.

The fix replaces the entire preflight auth resolution with delegation to `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()`, the same shared function used by the normal model request path. This ensures:
- Authorization header and custom-header auth are built identically in both paths
- Prefix trimming and header collision cleanup apply automatically
- `request.headers` are always forwarded (even without `request.auth`)

**Real environment tested:**
- **OS:** Ubuntu 24.04 (Oracle VPS)
- **Runtime:** Node.js v24.14.0
- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.5.18 (npm install)
- **Provider:** LiteLLM proxy at http://127.0.0.1:4000
- **Auth method:** LITELLM_API_KEY environment variable

**Exact steps or command run after this patch (original base auth case):**
```bash
node -e "const m = require('./dist/index.cjs'); m.probeCronModelProvider({ provider: 'litellm', model: 'gpt-4o' }).then(r => console.log(JSON.stringify(r)))"
```

**Evidence after fix:** LiteLLM proxy logs before (top) and after (bottom) the patch show GET /models now gets 200 OK instead of 401:

![Before/after terminal comparison](https://github.com/samson1357924/openclaw/releases/download/evd-pr83648/evidence-pr-img.jpg)

Before — no auth header, cron preflight triggers 401:
```
LiteLLM Proxy:ERROR - Exception occured - No api key passed in.
```

After — auth header sent, preflight succeeds:
```
127.0.0.1:45914 - "GET /models HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
```

The comprehensive auth normalization refactoring (prefix trim, header merging, parity with normal path) is covered by the test suite below.

**Additional verification (unit test suite):**
```
 ✓ src/cron/isolated-agent/model-preflight.runtime.test.ts (31 tests) 575ms
 ✓ src/agents/provider-request-config.test.ts (30 tests) 62ms
All 61 tests pass across both files. No regressions.

```
Coverage includes:
- 4 cross-path auth header parity tests (preflight headers match normal path headers)
- 4 edge case tests (empty header fields, unknown mode, whitespace token, OAuth marker)
- Existing bearer auth, header mode, and fallback tests

**Observed result after fix:**
- Live LiteLLM probe: GET /models changed from 401 Unauthorized to 200 OK
- Unit tests: 31/31 pass (preflight), 30/30 pass (regression)
- Code review: no Critical or Important issues found across all 5 dimensions
- Auth delegation is correct with proper fallback semantics and edge case handling

**What was not tested:**
- Integration test with actual local provider endpoint (preflight tests mock `fetchWithSsrFGuard`)
- `prepareProviderRuntimeAuth` plugin auth exchange path (documented gap — preflight intentionally skips this for local provider probes)
- Provider-level headers (`providerConfig.headers`) — these require SecretInput resolution not available at preflight's raw config stage

target.title: fix(memory): resolve embedding provider by authProviderId fallback
target.body:
## Summary

- When a user configures `memorySearch.provider` to `"google"`, the embedding provider lookup failed with "Unknown memory embedding provider: google" because the registered adapter id is `"gemini"` while the config-level provider name is `"google"`.
- The gemini memory embedding adapter (`extensions/google/memory-embedding-adapter.ts`) declares `authProviderId: "google"`.
- This fix adds an `authProviderId` fallback once in `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider`, the shared runtime resolver in `src/plugins/memory-embedding-provider-runtime.ts`, so all consumers (memory manager, Gateway `/v1/embeddings`, startup warnings, doctor checks) agree on the resolution.
- No duplicated call-site scans — the fallback lives in the single shared resolver.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts` → 8 passed (includes regression test for "google" → "gemini")
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts` → 36 passed
- Real production-path proof: registered the actual gemini adapter and verified resolution (see below)

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / [ ] Feature / [x] Refactor / [ ] Docs / [ ] Security / [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / [ ] Skills / [ ] Auth / [x] Memory / [ ] Integrations / [ ] API / [ ] UI/UX / [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `memorySearch.provider: "google"` caused "Unknown memory embedding provider: google" in `--index` and `--deep` because the gemini adapter id ("gemini") did not match the config-level provider name ("google"). The fix adds a centralized `authProviderId` fallback so "google" resolves to the registered gemini adapter.

**Environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, `node --import tsx` calling the actual production `registerMemoryEmbeddingProvider` + `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider` from the fix branch with the real gemini adapter from `extensions/google/memory-embedding-adapter.ts`.

**Steps run after the patch:** Registered the production gemini adapter (mirroring what the Google plugin does at gateway startup), then called `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider("google")` — the exact code path that `--index` and `--deep` hit when `memorySearch.provider` is "google".

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
Registered gemini adapter:
  id: gemini
  authProviderId: google
  defaultModel: gemini-embedding-001

=== Reproducing #90786: memorySearch.provider = "google" ===

After fix: "google" resolves to adapter "gemini"
  -> This is the fix. Before, this returned undefined and threw:
     "Unknown memory embedding provider: google"

"gemini" direct lookup: OK

"nonexistent" lookup: undefined (correct)
```

**Observed result after the fix:** `getMemoryEmbeddingProvider("google")` correctly resolves to the gemini adapter via the centralized `authProviderId` fallback. Direct lookup by adapter id ("gemini") continues to work. Unknown providers return `undefined` with no false positives.

**Not tested:** Live Rocky Linux gateway with real Google AI Studio API key, full `openclaw memory status --index --agent main` run, and memory index rebuild.

Closes #90786

target.title: fix(discord/security-audit): include top-level allowFrom in ownerAllowFromConfigured
target.body:
## Summary

The `channels.discord.commands.native.no_allowlists` audit warning is supposed to fire when a Discord operator hasn't configured any allowlist that would let users invoke `/…` slash commands. Today it only consults legacy `channels.discord.dm.allowFrom` plus the credential store when computing `ownerAllowFromConfigured`, even though:

- the audit's own remediation text tells users to add their id to `channels.discord.allowFrom` (the documented preferred path), and
- runtime authorization in `extensions/discord/src/monitor/native-command.ts` already honors top-level `allowFrom` via `discordConfig?.allowFrom ?? discordConfig?.dm?.allowFrom ?? []`.

So a user who follows the audit's own remediation sees the warning persist no matter where they put their id.

This PR makes the audit match runtime: `ownerAllowFromConfigured` now also includes top-level `discordCfg.allowFrom`, normalized through `normalizeAllowFromList` like the existing inputs.

Fixes #39341. Earlier triage at #67222 (closed as duplicate by `clawsweeper`).

## Changes

- `extensions/discord/src/security-audit.ts` — read `discordCfg.allowFrom` alongside `dm.allowFrom` and the credential store; pass all three through `normalizeAllowFromList`.
- `extensions/discord/src/security-audit.test.ts` — add a parameterized regression case asserting that a top-level `allowFrom` snowflake suppresses the warning, alongside the existing `dm.allowFrom` case.
- `CHANGELOG.md` — entry under `Unreleased → Fixes`.

Account-scoped paths (`channels.discord.accounts.<id>.allowFrom`) flow through the same code path because `params.account.config` already resolves to the account's config object before this function runs; no separate handling needed.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/security-audit.test.ts` — 8/8 pass (was 7; +1 new regression case).
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck + lint + import-cycles all green for changed scope.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` on touched files — clean.

## Not in scope

- The `dangerouslyAllowNameMatching` / name-based-entries check at `:108-129` already reads top-level `allowFrom` (line 64) — only the `ownerAllowFromConfigured` calculation downstream was missing it, so this is a one-spot fix.
- No remediation-text changes; the existing text was already correct, the audit's *check* was the part out of sync.

Reproducibility: yes. Running `pnpm build` on a Node binary where `process.features.typescript` is false is the high-confidence path; current main leaves build-all strip-types args unchanged, and the PR body supplies before/after proof from such a host.
